title: Revisiting Bloodborne: The Board Game - This Hunter's Dream is a frustrating one
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UqLF2eIaDxg

By request, Daniel (@dungeondive) revisits Bloodborne: The Board Game to see whether his opinion has softened — and it hasn't. He still finds it well-made and full of things he likes, which is exactly why it frustrates him so much. Playing the Secrets of the Church campaign with the Blade of Mercy and his beloved Kirkhammer, he again praises the lore-rich scenario setup, the short and well-paced campaign system with its branching cards and analog memory, the variety of enemies across his Kickstarter-plus-expansions collection, the deck-building upgrades, and the strong table presence.

But the play still stresses him out. He tries a BGG "Adventure mode" house rule (a four-card hand instead of three) which eases the tight economy a little, yet the core problems remain: enemies respawn simply by taking turns rather than at your choosing, the game ends at an arbitrary, un-thematic point on the track, and — most damning — the board game rewards cautious, defensive play and avoiding combat, the opposite of the aggressive, regain-fueled video game. Engaging with the best part feels like a punishment. With too many other games to enjoy, he's selling his Bloodborne collection.

----

Daniel's biggest gripe is that the Bloodborne board game rewards the *opposite* playstyle of its video-game source. When an adaptation contradicts the feel of its original, does that break it for you?
